title: Mint Chutney (Pudina Chutney)

ingredients.title: Ingredients
items: Fresh mint leaves – 1 tightly packed cup (washed, stems mostly removed)
Fresh coriander (cilantro) leaves – 1 tightly packed cup (washed, tender stems ok)
Black salt (kala namak) – ¼ tsp (optional, for an earthy tang)
Cold water – 2–3 tbsp (or as needed for consistency)

instruction.title: Method
steps: Prep the herbs: Pluck the mint leaves and rinse them in cold water to remove grit. Pat dry gently. Wash coriander with stems (trim thick ends), pat dry.

Blend for freshness: In a high-speed blender, add mint, coriander, green chilies, ginger, garlic (if using), lemon juice, cumin powder, black salt, regular salt, and sugar. Add 1–2 tbsp cold water to help blending — cold water prevents discoloration and keeps flavors bright.
Process until smooth: Blend until the chutney is completely smooth and creamy. Stop and scrape down the sides as needed. Adjust thickness by adding a splash more cold water if needed.

Taste & balance: Check seasoning — you should taste freshness from mint, citrus tang from lemon, heat from chilies, and a subtle earthy spice from cumin. If it’s slightly bitter (common with mint), a pinch more sugar will balance it.

Serve or store: Serve immediately with samosas, pakoras, kebabs, sandwiches, or as a spread. Store in an airtight container in the fridge for up to 3 days. For longer storage, freeze in ice cube trays.

chefTips: Ice cube blending trick: Add a small ice cube instead of water when blending — it prevents heat from the blender motor from dulling the green color.
Flavor layering: If using with chaat or street food, mix in a little yogurt (50:50) for a creamier, milder dip.
Avoid metallic taste: Don’t store in reactive metal containers; use glass or food-grade plastic.
